title: Retromod
description: Retromod Is a Minecraft mod that allows you to play older mods on newer versions.
body: **This mod is the work of just me (and Github helpers) on a project that I thought could solve one of my biggest complaints, mod compatibility between versions. So I tried to solve it, it is not complete yet so if you can go to [Github](https://github.com/Bownlux/RetroMod) to improve this mod that would be so helpful to me and the community. If you dont know how to code or just want to donate you can just buy stuff from my server, RevivalSMP's Store https://play.revivalsmp.net/store and get stuff in return. This is getting super long so thank you for reading this and now, onto retromod.** --- # Retromod **Run old Minecraft mods on new Minecraft. No manual fixes needed.** - **Drop-in:** put Retromod in `mods/`, put your old mods in `retromod-input/`, launch. It rewrites old mods on the fly so they work on current Minecraft. - **Loaders:** Fabric, NeoForge, Forge. Translates mods from **1.12.2 and up** onto **Minecraft 1.20 – 26.2**. - **On MC 26.2, use the OpenGL renderer.** Vulkan is 26.2's new default and breaks old mods' rendering — Retromod switches you to OpenGL automatically (Video Settings → Graphics API → OpenGL to confirm). - **Status:** 1.1.0 is stable; **1.2.0-snapshot-1** is the current snapshot, this is where the newest 26.x compatibility work lands. Keep backups of your mod files. - **Not for** deep-integration mods like Create or OptiFine — they replace Minecraft's rendering, which no automated tool can bridge. - [Compatibility DB](https://bownlux.github.io/Retromod/compatdb/) · [Report issues](https://github.com/Bownlux/Retromod/issues) > **Fabric is the primary loader.** Retromod runs on Fabric, NeoForge, and Forge, but Fabric is the loader I personally use and test most, so it has the most complete coverage and the fastest fixes. NeoForge is close behind. Forge support exists but is the least mature; older Forge mods (especially anything compiled with SRG-name obfuscation) often need extra work that's still on the roadmap. Picking a loader fresh? **Pick Fabric** for the smoothest experience. > **1.1.0 is the current stable release; 1.2.0 is currently getting made, if you want the new features, choose 1.2.0, if you just want to use a versions of Retromod you know your mods work with, then stay on 1.1.0 --- [![Revival Worlds](https://revivalworlds.com/resources/revival-worlds-banner.png)](https://revivalworlds.com) --- ## What it does Have a mod from MC 1.18 you still love? Retromod rewrites it so it works on MC 26.2. No downloading newer versions, no asking the mod author to update, no giving up on your favorite pack. - Translates old mods to new Minecraft automatically, from Minecraft 1.12.2 (2017) all the way through 26.2 - Works with Fabric, NeoForge, and Forge, so use whatever loader you like - Handles the hard stuff: method renames, class moves, removed APIs, broken mixins, version-range rejections - Keeps your originals safe. The mods you drop in are backed up, the transformed copies go in `mods/` - In-game mods menu: click the gear icon on the title screen to see what Retromod did to each mod - Fast. It uses all your CPU cores briefly during the first launch, then sits out of the way ### Mods it knows about Retromod has built-in support for **34+ popular mods and modding APIs**, so things like JEI, REI, Cloth Config, Trinkets, Curios, GeckoLib, Architectury, Create, Mod Menu, Jade, and more get extra-special handling. Using a more obscure mod? The general translation pipeline still applies, and most mods translate fine even without specific handling. **Some legacy mods even get automatically shimmed to their modern equivalents:** | Old mod | Works as if you had | |---|---| | Baubles | Curios | | NEI (Not Enough Items) | JEI | | Thermal Expansion / RF | Forge Energy | | Old WAILA | Jade | --- ## Supported Minecraft versions **You need MC 1.20 or newer** to run Retromod itself. Retromod will then translate old mods FROM almost any earlier version: | Your Minecraft | Old mods it can translate | |---|---| | **Fabric** 1.20 – 26.2 | 1.14 and up | | **NeoForge** 1.20.1 – 26.2 | 1.20.1 and up | | **Forge** 1.20 – 26.1 | 1.12.2 and up (Java 8 era) | > **On Minecraft 26.2, use the OpenGL renderer.** 26.2 makes the new **Vulkan** backend the default, but translated old mods that do their own OpenGL rendering can glitch, render nothing, or crash on Vulkan — they need the OpenGL backend, which 26.2 still includes. Retromod switches you to OpenGL automatically the first time it runs on 26.2 (it won't override a renderer you chose yourself); to set it by hand, go to **Video Settings → Graphics API → OpenGL**. (On macOS this doesn't matter — Minecraft uses OpenGL there anyway.) Forge doesn't have a 26.2 build yet, so a Forge 26.2 file follows when it does. > ⚠️ **Primary target is Minecraft 26.1+.** > > Retromod works on Minecraft 1.20 – 1.21.x and keeps getting better there. The same improvements that go into the 26.1 version (performance, bug fixes, smarter mod handling) show up for you too. But **new mods added to the compatibility list** and **testing effort** are focused on Minecraft 26.1+. > > If you're on 1.20 or 1.21.x, Retromod is still a great choice. Upgrading to 26.1 just means you'll see brand-new compatibility coverage land sooner and for more mods. > > Why 26.1? It's the first Minecraft version Mojang released without code obfuscation, which is a huge deal for any mod-translation tool. We're pointing our attention at the version of Minecraft going forward from here. --- ## How to use it ### Fabric 1. Put the Retromod jar for your MC version (e.g. `retromod-1.1.0-rc.1+26.1.2.jar`) in your `mods/` folder. 2. Launch Minecraft, then close it. (This creates the `retromod-input/` folder.) 3. Drop your old mods into `retromod-input/`. 4. Launch Minecraft again. Retromod translates your mods while loading. 5. **Close Minecraft and launch one more time.** Your old mods now work. The two-launch dance is a Fabric quirk. It checks mod versions *before* any mod (including Retromod) gets to run, so we need one launch to translate and one launch to actually load. ### Forge / NeoForge 1. Put Retromod in `mods/`. 2. Drop your old mods in `mods/` too, or in `retromod-input/` if you prefer. 3. Launch. If Retromod needs to translate anything, it'll prompt you to restart. 4. Done. Works on servers too. Players don't need Retromod installed to connect to a Retromod-using server (unless the server is loading client-side mods, which it shouldn't be). --- ## FAQ **What does "release candidate" / pre-release mean? Is it safe to install?** Yes. `1.1.0-rc.1` is a release candidate — the 1.1.0 work is feature-complete and we're shaking out edge cases in specific mod combinations before the stable tag. The core pipeline (the part that rewrites your mod files) is well-tested and doesn't touch anything outside the Retromod folders. Backups are always a good idea with any tool that modifies files, but that's a general habit, not a warning specific to the pre-release. **Where exactly do I put my old mods?** In the `retromod-input/` folder inside your `.minecraft/` directory (the one that also contains `mods/`, `saves/`, `options.txt`, etc.). Retromod creates this folder for you on first launch. If you can't find it, launch Minecraft once with Retromod installed and it'll appear. Heads-up: drop the mods directly in `retromod-input/` itself, **not** in its `processed/` subfolder. `processed/` is the *after-transform* staging area where Retromod moves originals once it's done with them, so anything you put in there is treated as already-handled and skipped on the next scan. (Retromod also scans a second copy at `.minecraft/mods/retromod-input/` if you have one, same rule there.) **Why does Minecraft have to restart?** Fabric in particular checks every mod's declared Minecraft version before any mod gets to run, so Retromod can't intercept that check on the same launch. We have to translate first, restart, and then Fabric sees the translated versions. Forge and NeoForge work the same way in practice even though the technical reason is different. **Does it work with OptiFine?** Not reliably. Most OptiFine features have modern alternatives that work better with Retromod: **Sodium** for performance, **Iris** for shaders, **Continuity** for connected textures, **Zoomify** for zoom. We recommend those instead. **Which renderer should I use on Minecraft 26.2?** **OpenGL.** Minecraft 26.2 added a new Vulkan renderer and made it the default, but old mods translated by Retromod often render incorrectly or crash on Vulkan — they need the OpenGL backend, which 26.2 still ships. Retromod switches you to OpenGL automatically the first time it runs on 26.2 (and leaves your choice alone if you picked one). To set it yourself: **Video Settings → Graphics API → OpenGL**. On macOS it doesn't matter (Minecraft uses OpenGL there regardless). **Does it work with Quilt?** Yes. Install the **Fabric** build of Retromod into your Quilt instance. Quilt Loader runs Fabric mods natively, so the Fabric JAR works on Quilt unchanged. No separate "Quilt build" is needed, and you don't have to translate anything beforehand to put it there. Quilt-native mods you want to translate (the ones that ship with `quilt.mod.json` instead of `fabric.mod.json`) are handled too, since Retromod recognizes both formats. **Does it work with Create / Tinkers / Applied Energistics 2 / other huge tech mods?** No, and probably not ever. Mods like **Create** touch thousands of MC API points across rendering, physics, networking, world generation, and animation, and ship their own rendering libraries (Flywheel for Create) that include native shader and GL code. Retromod rewrites Java bytecode. It can't translate native code, and it can't synthesize new mixin injection points where the underlying bytecode has been entirely rewritten. The same applies to Applied Energistics 2, Tinkers' Construct, IndustrialCraft, and any mod that meaningfully replaces MC's rendering pipeline. For these you genuinely need the original mod author to ship a port for the new MC version; no automated tool can bridge that gap. Retromod is the right tool for content mods, QoL mods, recipe viewers, equipment slots, and most "normal" mods, not for deep-integration tech overhauls. **Some of my old mod's features stopped working. Why?** Sometimes an old mod hooks into Minecraft internals that have been completely rewritten. Retromod can usually keep the mod *loaded* even when a specific hook is broken, but that one feature won't do anything. For example, an old item-tooltip mod might load but show nothing new on tooltips. We're adding coverage for these cases in future updates. **My game crashes on startup / when making a new world.** Paste your log in a GitHub issue. Most launch crashes are one of a small set of known issues we're fixing. The crash log tells us exactly which mod hit a problem so we can add targeted handling. **Does Retromod slow down Minecraft?** Only briefly. When you first launch with new mods in `retromod-input/`, Retromod spikes your CPU for 1–5 seconds to translate them. After that it does almost nothing, and your framerate won't change. **My computer is slow / low on RAM.** Add `-Dretromod.parallelism=2` to your Minecraft launcher's JVM arguments. That tells Retromod to use 2 CPU cores instead of all of them, which uses less memory during translation. Translation will take a bit longer (maybe 10–20 seconds instead of 3) but everything still works. **Something's broken / I found a bug.** Please report it on [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/Bownlux/Retromod/issues) with your log file. We triage fast, and being a release candidate means we want to hear about problems. **I'm a mod author and I don't want Retromod touching my mod.** We hear you. Add an empty file at `META-INF/retromod-opt-out` inside your mod's JAR (or the `src/main/resources/META-INF/retromod-opt-out` path in your source tree, which the build will package). Retromod sees the marker, logs "Skipping `` — mod author opted out," and copies your JAR through to the mods folder unchanged. No transformation runs, ever. Full details are in our [README](https://github.com/Bownlux/Retromod/blob/main/README.md#opting-your-mod-out-of-retromod-transformation). --- ## Uninstalling - **Fabric:** Remove `retromod-*.jar` from `mods/` and delete `config/fabric_loader_dependencies.json`. - **Forge / NeoForge:** Remove `retromod-*.jar` from `mods/`. If anything looks off after uninstalling, your original mod files are in `mods/retromod-backups/`. --- ## Badge for Translated Mods If you used Retromod to translate your mod to a newer version, you can optionally add this badge to your mod page description. For CurseForge, just copy the image with the link; for Modrinth and GitHub, use the Markdown or HTML snippet. [![Translated with Retromod](https://img.shields.io/badge/Translated_with-Retromod-blue?logo=data:image/png;base64,)](https://modrinth.com/mod/retromod) **Markdown:** ``` [![Translated with Retromod](https://img.shields.io/badge/Translated_with-Retromod-blue?logo=data:image/png;base64,)](https://modrinth.com/mod/retromod) ``` **HTML:** ```html ``` --- ## Links - **Source & Issues:** [github.com/Bownlux/Retromod](https://github.com/Bownlux/Retromod) - **License:** [MIT](LICENSE) --- > *Power-user note: there's also a standalone CLI tool for transforming mods outside of Minecraft, generating per-mod compatibility reports, and batch-processing whole mod folders. Most people don't need it, since the in-game mod handles everything automatically. If you want it anyway, grab it from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/Bownlux/Retromod/releases) (`retromod-X.X.X-cli.jar`) and run it with `java -jar retromod-X.X.X-cli.jar help`.* --- ## Info on AI usage This project has used AI to help with development, but not so much. 80% of the code is what I wrote, but I do get AI to make commits on what the code does and also to refine and fix my code if it breaks. I do review every change an AI makes to ensure quality and make sure it isn't breaking stuff while fixing things. --- Made by **Bownlux**, part of the team behind **[RevivalSMP.net](https://revivalsmp.net)** ---
